mainEntity.name: Does coffee origin really change the taste?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, more than any other single factor before roasting. Altitude, climate, soil, and the local processing tradition set a coffee's acidity, body, and base flavors. A high-grown washed Ethiopian tastes floral and bright. A wet-hulled Sumatran tastes earthy and heavy. Same plant, completely different cup.

Single origin coffee comes from one source: one country, and often one region, farm, or single harvest, rather than a blend of beans from several countries. The point is to show the character of that one place. Blends instead mix origins for consistency and a repeatable flavor.

mainEntity.name: Which coffee origin is best for beginners?

acceptedAnswer.text: Colombia and Nicaragua are the most forgiving starting points. Both deliver mild acidity, medium body, and clear caramel and chocolate notes that survive brewing mistakes. Save high-acid Ethiopian light roasts for once you know what brightness you like. Start smooth, then explore toward the extremes.
